author: @Fridge_Fiend
text:
That's objectively not true lol

Like ABL on big oled tv's is a none issue, this is especially true cause these videos are almost always SDR not HDR so there's no peak brightness

Real reason is they're in wide color gamut mode and playing SDR content made for that

Basically no SDR content is made for WCG, that's all hdr, so it's gonna look really broken when you leave it in WCG for normal sdr content

author: @animenion.
text:
Incase you wondering how these small things are made? 

Making transistors requires an extreme process called photolithography. Because they are too small to be crafted with physical tools, manufacturers essentially "print" them using extreme ultraviolet (EUV) light. Light is shined through a microscopic stencil onto a silicon wafer, and chemical baths then etch away the unexposed silicon

text: Edge compute makes perfect sense. Balancing packaging, performance to cost ratio, and weatherproofing simultaneously is a big problem with traditional nodes. And there are quite a few that I can think of.

author: @vonx_money
text: Modern integral Polaroid instant film doesn’t need to be shaken, correct. The shaking practice stems from Polaroid Pack film which hasn’t been produced for 1-2+ decades. Integral film is a self-contained packet where reagent chemical is spread across your exposed negatives where it then develops into a positive right there in the print. Pack film came before Integral film and used a similar process where reagent chemicals are squeezed across your print by rollers to develop. However, once developed the image didn’t appear on it’s own, you had to peel apart where the negative and the positive touched each other with chemicals inbetween. Once you peeled the film apart you’d have your negative, and your positive (your print). That positive still had a chemical goo on it that you’d need to dry off, either by shaking or air dry
video_title: STOP shaking your Polaroid film 📸 #learningnewthings #polaroidphoto #polaroidcamera

text: I worked with the company that helped machine the prototype parts for the Apple car. I hate that it works, but it does. 
To all the people reading this: when someone is asking a question in the comments please respond. Just like you would respond to someone being wrong.
video_title: Is this camera "faster than light"?

author: @fuby6065
text:
probably worth noting that even if there was a physical shutter for each frame, it wouldn't have to be faster than light. As long as the distance the shutter travels per frame, is less than the distance the light travels per frame, you're in the clear. Speed involves a distance too! Not just time. Even if there were things that had to move really fast every frame (there isn't really, or the electrons I suppose), all that matters is they move less distance per frame than the light moves. I know you know this, but worth stating probably.

As you say, the units don't compare, if you wanna compare, add a distance, no part of the camera moves more distance per frame, than the light moves per frame, so it's all fine.

text: I mean, part of how this works in the first place is that you're massively undersampling a periodic process, leading to temporal aliasing that makes it look like the same beam (wave packet? pulse? Edit: It's a really cool solution to a seemingly-impossible problem.

Okay so this is what happened for all those interested:

So Windows 11 requires the Trusted Platform Module to run. Most likely, it was enabled on the BIOS level then Windows 10 was upgraded to Windows 11.

However, the user must have unplugged the PC and left it unplugged for a couple days and, with the CMOS battery dead, those settings disappeared. Therefore, the system would no longer boot as TPM was now disabled again.

author: @Prismatic8000
text:
1/2 cup (115 g) butter
1 cup (200 g) sugar
2 eggs
1 tsp vanilla extract
1/3 cup (40 g) cocoa powder
1/2 cup (65 g) all-purpose flour
1/4 tsp salt
Optional: 1/2 cup chocolate chips or chopped nuts
Instructions
Preheat oven to 350°F (175°C).
Grease or line an 8×8 inch (20×20 cm) baking pan.
Melt the butter in a bowl.
Stir in the sugar.
Mix in the eggs and vanilla.
Add cocoa powder, flour, and salt. Stir until combined.
Fold in chocolate chips or nuts if using.
Pour batter into the pan and spread evenly.
Bake for 20–25 minutes.
Let cool before cutting into squares.
video_title: Building a Custom Keyboard

author: @Mr_Pryor
text: That’s not really how phone batteries work. The mAh rating is mainly the battery’s capacity, meaning how much charge it can store, not how much power it “forces” into the phone. Using a higher or lower mAh battery won’t automatically damage the phone as long as the voltage, connector, physical fit, battery chemistry, and protection circuitry are compatible. The real risk is using a cheap or incompatible battery, not simply using a different mAh rating.

text:
Happened to me, with Samsung, randomly said there is moisture in the charging port.

I tried keeping it in Rice, silica gel, and also cleaned it with Isopropyl Alcohol, still shwped the error.

as a last ditch effort.

I warmed my oven to 40C, and then unplugged to oven and kept the phone in it for like 40 minutes.

solved the issue.

text: Nokia 3310 - I have two - one I still use every day the other is a spare - both 26 years old. My everyday one is still using its original SIM card from 2000 - but has had about 6 new batteries over that time. Only has a 2G connection for basic calls and texts - no camera or internet. Should continue to work until 2G gets switched off around 2030. Battery easily lasts 3 days on stand-bye and 1-2 days with basic use. Will be sad when it gets disconnected from the network.

author: @lottalotto691
text:
1. Satellites track your location, plus other tools in the phone to estimate your location while driving.
2. Traffic data is pooled mostly from other people using the same gps app or apps using the same data broker. 
3. Trends are developed over time to best calculate routes, traffic, and timing.
4. Map data is mostly open source meaning there are many contributors from all around the world to fill in street names, businesses, events, etc.
5. Routes are calculated with weighted pathing algorithms like A* (a-star), and fine tuned based on trends, like popular routes and traffic.

A few factual mistakes, sorry about that!

  - The RV1126 SoC used in JetKVM and some clones is not, in fact, RISC-V, it is an Arm-based SoC (still saves a lot in cost over integrating a Raspberry Pi!)
  - GL-iNet also has a self-hosted dashboard for managing multiple Comets (I will have to test it!)
  - The FBI will (probably) not visit you for watching this video. You've been (not) warned!

author: @paintenzero
text:
6:46 JetKVM uses cheap Rockchip RV1106 cpu, it is ARM chip created for video processing. So, not a RISC-V chip.
Luckfox is the only company creating SDK for that RV1106 chip. The CPU is pretty specific and you can't use mainline bootloader and Linux kernel. So JetKVM used Luckfox SDK to create the OS. That's why it was so easy for Luckfox to copy the whole thing.

author: @lordbalzamore7692
text: One upgrade I don't hear often is ergonomics. A good seat, with a desk at the right height and a monitor at the right angle and distance will set your body up to do insane movements that you didn't even know you were capable of. If you can aim from the shoulder, your arm starts to feel like a mechanical arm (this is also correct drawing technique). Optimal monitor distance is a function of your optical prescription too, so getting an eye test is also a bit of an upgrade haha, I don't need glasses but I still have a slight prescription which means the monitor should be further away than if I wore glasses, and the difference on eye fatigue is noticeable.
video_title: The best (and worst) setup upgrades.

author: @lutrine
text:
here's the hierarchy:

1. *gpu* (0:27) — higher frame rate and lower latency between frames
2. *monitor* (2:02) — allows you to see said lower latency; he said that 360 Hz is where diminishing returns start to become apparent
3. *keyboard* (3:29) — upgrading from a mechanical keyboard to an _analog_ one removes the travel time required to register an input
4. *mouse* (5:05) — most of the time lighter = better, but at a certain point it becomes preference
5. *cpu* (6:40) — upgrading this can widen the bottleneck for your GPU, allowing it to push out more frames per second
6. *mousepad* (8:59) — almost all preference, but if you play tracking-based games, you should try out a glass mousepad with a gaming arm sleeve
7. *headphones* (9:51) — preference
8. *ram* (10:30) — try to get the cheapest 32 GB 6000 MHz set you can if you're on an AM5 motherboard or similar

hope this summary helps! :)
video_title: The best (and worst) setup upgrades.

author: @SyntaxBear
text: I am an IT admin, and this video is littered with falsehoods and embellishments about what Teams is actually capable of. There is a difference between Bossware and tools like Microsoft 365. Archiving and reviewing messages are required for most companies, and it's not automatic for people to search through messages. Only IT admins with certain permissions can even view the data, given how sensitive it is. The vast majority of telemetry data is useless for accurately reflecting activity. It's intended to help IT improve system performance, and any other use wouldn't stand in court with the right representation. The best practice is to keep business separate from personal devices just for your own sake.

author: @sf-jim8885
text: I've seen the reverse happen on a professional monitor, where the screen was blank, but if I bounced a flashlight off the screen, I could see a very dim picture, which meant that all the video circuits were working OK but the CFL backlight was out. With some simple troubleshooting I figured out that the CFL driver board was bad. These driver boards are fairly standard among many brands of TV's & monitors. I found the part number stenciled on the circuit board, and ordered an identical one online, and that fixed the problem. So, for $45 I fixed a professional monitor that it would have taken almost $4k to replace. My boss was very happy.

author: @albinoethiopian3
text:
I tried it for a day. Mostly used it for youtube. There were some things I really liked about it (the highlight/right click/search google popup-window was really cool). But the lag was unbearable!!! I click to pause a youtube video, and 10-15 seconds later it pauses. Same for playing. It was also using 5,000 to 6,000 MB of RAM with like 10 tabs open. I'm back in Vivaldi now with 73 tabs in my current workspace, and like 30 other workspaces with tons of tabs sorted into them. It's currently running about 1,000 MB RAM. 
I'm gonna be sticking with Vivaldi for now until Zen can get their RAM mess cleaned up, but Zen definitely has potential

author: @MasterHigure
text:
My favorite counterintuitive orbital fact is that a gas cloud (which is gravitationally bound, and therefore a huge amount of particles more or less in orbit around one another) heats up as you remove energy from it. When a star forms inside one and literally starts blasting the cloud with enormous amounts of energy, it cools down.

It's basically the "higher orbit means lower speed" thing, but for a LOT of particles at the same time.

author: @harryf9885
text:
1:52 it is important to note that accelerating towards an object in the same orbit and then decelerating once you get there will work if your craft has enough fuel to do it, and this is indeed what spacecraft do to make it the last little bit of distance to link up with each other. It’s just that doing it that way is really inefficient and becomes more inefficient exponentially the weaker your rocket’s thrust is and the farther away you start because you are fighting orbital mechanics the whole time you are trying get there.  The reason for the exponentially increasing inefficiency is pretty easy to understand: You are in an orbit and your target is in an orbit and therefore your relative position is constantly changing so the target is always moving away from where you were rocketing towards a second ago, so now you are going the wrong way and have to turn and fight against the momentum going in the wrong direction that you yourself spent fuel to create, so the longer it takes the worse it is. Because we have weak rockets with limited fuel, we always want to take a path that takes advantage of orbital mechanics almost the whole way rather than fighting them the whole way, then only use the simple and obvious method of getting closer for the final docking when the vehicles are already so close to each other that we hardly get any benefit from trying to take advantage of orbital mechanics the rest of the way, and hardly any hinderance from fighting them, so we can use the simple method of pointing towards where we want to go to get it done faster and with more precision. Interestingly, beyond the simplest path and the most efficient path there is also a fastest path, in which you head directly towards a point ahead of the vehicle you are behind of on its orbit such that you arrive there before the other vehicle does. Just enough ahead of it that you can cancel your relative velocity by the time you both get there so you are back on the same orbit and don’t get shot out in a highly elliptical one. The more powerful your rocket or the closer you are to your target, the more the fastest path looks like the simplest path because you have to aim less ahead of the other rocket to get there and slow down in time. The weaker your rocket and the further you are from the target, the more the fastest path looks like the most efficient path, because you have to lead by so much that the place where you need to meet the rocket is on its next orbit and is behind you so you have to propel yourself in the opposite direction if you want to go towards it and thus you will drop into a lower orbit to get there first. 


Understanding that there is a simplest path, a fastest path, a most efficient path, and the relationship between them I think removes a lot of what is counterintuitive about the most efficient path. Namely that many people think based on how it’s presented that you must do it that way, and that is counterintuitive because it pretty obviously isn’t true if you think about it.
video_title: Two Paradoxes of Space Navigation

text: Any Costco price that ends with .97 indicates it is a clearance item.  Typically, those clearance items have a significant price reduction.  However, items are put on clearance by the individual store managers, meaning that one store might have the item on clearance, but other Costcos might have the exact same item at the regular Costco retail price.

author: @ChRisu_Ojisan
text: The costco edition will bring something extra that the retail doesn't. Costco buyers will ask for upgrades. The manufacturers don't really like them. The B-line rumor is bullshit. I have friends that work for both sides (costco buyers and manufacturers) like sony and samsung. They are not B-line or refurbished items. I work for costco by the way 29 years and counting. Let me give you an example. On the Sony Oleds from a few years ago the 80K. The costco version had a button on the bottom to turn off the mic. On the high end TCL TVs would bring better quality speakers. On headphones Costco will ask for a free case, or cables etc. A lot of buyers hate this but they know the buying power Costco has so they usually will do it for some items. They present Costco buyers their whole line up but Costco will only pick items that make sense. Items they think will sell well with their members. At the same time some Costcos get items others don't. The top selling Costco's will get more high end items not avaliable at stores that don't perform as well. Buy electronics with confidence because Costco buyers will buy only the best of the best for the stores to carry items that make sense for their members. That mac ending in .97 cents is a pending delete item so this is why is cheaper. We try to get rid of it to bring the new version in. We try not to keep old items in stores. We never sell open box unless is a display item.
video_title: Why is Costco Tech SO Cheap?

author: @CaryGordon3k
text: I used to work for an electronics manufacturer that sold their products in Costco as well. At the time the only difference between the model sold at Costco and the standard model was the model number. The thing is that the warranty was doubled when sold through Costco, which was immediately adjusted for in our ticketing system when repair tickets were opened. When items couldn't be repaired Costco immediately swapped out for a replacement unit and our RMA was done through the Costco, super convenient.

author: @3lectr1c
text:
Hi Clint! I run the MacDat site you showed and also own a Lite 4/25E (along with three other LTE Lite models) - here's some notes which might help you out with yours:

- The plastics are surprisingly hardy on these. They're very deceptive, because they *do* like to crack around the hinges, but when I actually took a pair of pliers to a junk display shell, they have a surprising amount of flex left. Either way, you should have no trouble getting the palmrest/keyboard surround off without damaging it. Those clips will put up a fight and be quite the pain, but they didn't break on any of my four units when I took them apart to pull the batteries out.

- The tunnel vision defect is insanely common on these displays. There's probably at least a 75% chance that the replacement display you ordered will have it as well. Baking at 212F for several hours will reduce or even eliminate the issue. Worth doing because as you said in the video, they're BEAUTIFUL displays. I really wish they were used in more computers than they were!

- Floppy drive is a Citizen V1DA and the problem is that they use a belt to drive the spindle motor. You can fix the drive by replacing the belt, but I don't currently have a source for V1DA belts. Your best option may be to order one of those misc belt kits for VCR/Tape decks and hope one fits.

- The CR2430 CMOS battery doesn't leak, but these also have a suspend battery inside which is NiCad and does leak. It's located inside the metal motherboard cage (you'll know what I mean when you get it apart). Definitely remove it when you have the computer apart.

- The AC adapter may need recapping in the future. There is also one capacitor on the trackball PCB which should be replaced.

I've got more detailed info, including a link to the original service manual, on the LTE Lite MacDat page (which you've probably found already, but I thought I'd mention it just in case). Loved the video :)
video_title: Obscure $3,000 Laptop From 1993: Compaq LTE Lite 4/25E

author: @valliantsteed
text:
Glad to see these on your channel. I have 2 LTE Elite 4/40cxs with color LCD , dead floppys on both of 'em with a stretched belt. Wierd "Citizen W1D" slim drives with non standard flat cable connectors. 

There was a Go-tek like emulator for a music keyboard that had the same drive and there was the OpenFlops-W1D. But I don't think it's still sold. I used a PCMCIA to CF Industrial and a  CF Industrial to IDE adapter as a removable drive and system drive as well as a 10mbit PCMCIA networkadapter. 

Nowadays, I have the elite dock but the lower case is shattered in shipping. I have to agree, lovely display on these.


In the future I hope to get YYZKevins PicoPCMCIA card running in it when I get it. Like the PicoMem and PicoGUS but for old laptops w/PCMCIA Cardbus. Sound on these will be sweet.

author: @mokkaherrman1104
text:
Explanation from a computerscientist: 

The general concept of devices like GPUs go back to the "Flynn Taxanomy" which is a theoretical model from all the way back in 1966, to classify computer architectures. This classification basically considers two questions: 

1. Can there be parallel instructions (single instruction or SI / multiple instruction or MI)

2. Can one instruction operate on multiple pieces of data at the same time (single data SD or multiple data MD)

When you hear this you may think "of course I want multiple instructions and multiple data" because multiplication sounds good for throughput, but reality is a bit more complicated.

As previously said, this is a design choice inherent to computers, so deciding for a SI/MD architecture, as GPUs do, means for the most part, that you can ONLY make calculations on big amounts of data. 

The problem about shaders and high-resolution screens though, is generally not that the calculations themselves are very complex, but rather that they have to be made on massive amounts of data, making GPUs perfect for the task. There are other famous use cases for GPUs in multiple fields, some you certainly know of.

A CPU on the other hand, is designed to handle stuff like I/O: The input ports of your computer, your network card and network traffic, logic, generally stuff that needs a lot of control, and most you do on modern computers needs a lot of control. They have historically been SISD, but moved on to being a mix of SI/SD, SI/MD and MI/MD in recent times, due to the use of multi-core architectures with integrated SI/MD units, which have been called 'integrated GPUs' in the past, and more recently 'AI Chips' which further blur the lines between the two.

A modern CPU's L1 cache has about 1/15th the latency and 2.5-4x higher bandwith than VRAM on a GPU. When a GPU is as fast as an average car, then a CPU is a fighter jet at mach 3.

For me, I think the car analogy undercuts it a little bit. 

I rather like to think of a CPU as a distribution center, airport and harbour. It handles all of the logistics, loads product around, and communicates with different parties to optimize its performance. It has complete control over the airport and is by a long shot the fastest and most flexible unit on the computer.

A GPU is within the harbour and sometimes gets hit with a delivery task from the CPU. It loads up and sends out giant cargo ships across the sea, nowhere nearly as fast or coordinated as the CPU, but it can transport millions of tons of materials in one shipment. A CPU can transport people, packages, light-weight stuff, sure, but building materials, minerals, oil and food for the entire planet, that's something a CPU can't do. When it comes to how much mass is delivered, a modern GPU is the best thing there is.

author: @TheArcanis87
text:
23:30 I think at that 99.9% of light speed the pen would scatter through the earth, and lots of it would make it to the other side, not interacting and not really digging an hole. The faster it goes, the more would get to the other side without interacting. It's like neutrons have to be slowed down to interact with uranium in fission ractors. At those energies collisions become weird things with cross sections like particle colliders, and cross sections are energy depoendent.

Air compression wouldn't be a thing. Waves propagate at the speed of sound, that much faster than sound speed the event has happened before any meaningful wave even start propagating. From the POV of air (or crust and mantle material, it's all the same), it would be a column of plasma appearing out of nowhere, and from there waves do start propagating.

The pen would lose shape pretty quickly, metal can't tank more than a few interactions. From the POW of the pen, it's hit by a wall of air particles at near light speed in the upper atmosphere. But the forward speed is so fast that it would become a very narrow cone of particles barreling forward, someone smart could do calculations of the divergence angle but I suspect at atmosphere scale it would be a beam.

A 27g pen would hold 51TJ of energy, so around 12 Kiloton of TNT. A small nuke.

The nuke yield instead of beinc concentrated in a point, is diffused in a column as the pen scatters through. I think it would look like a bright beam doing a pulse of radiation with lots of gamma rays because of the particle interactions and air shower, with a thunder shockwave, but delivering surprisingly little destruction on the surface.

The video hints that energy increases exponentially as the object approaches light speed and can never get to it. The faster the object move through space, the slower it moves through time, and at lightspeed object are frozen in time and speed loses meaning. A curious side effect is that a radioactive particle traveling fast, will decay slower because of that.

Only massless object travel at light speed but cannot slow down.

author: @JohnSmith762A11B
text: I remember a hardware engineer decades ago telling a classroomn at MIT, "I hear people complaining about how often hard drives fail. Look, you should be amazed they EVER work..." Watching Anastasi talk about chip manufacturing makes me amazed my gadgets EVER work, and doubt any computers in the future—especially those expected to be made with even more complex processes—can be made to work at all. I really can't decide if her videos wreck or amplify my faith in humanity. These videos are great but they are also a form of technological horror film.

text: Interesting development if it happens regarding a single light source.  The first industrial revolution was powered by water wheels which spun a shaft running the length of the factory, often spinning machines for materials.  Power was taken from the shaft through large, flat belts to run the individual machines, spinning, lathes, etc. The first factory I worked in back in 1969 still had the shafts and belts on the ceiling although no longer used.  Everything old is new again.

text: Great episode! An earlier approach to FEL was to use Van de Graaff generators for the acceleration. Oxford University in the U.K had a proposal to covert a 10 million volt machine into an electron accelerator & then install wigler magnets to create photon beams. Sadly like many other UK science proposals it did not get funded. Interesting how diverse are the practical ideas for FEL across so many nations. & yet more interesting is that the current leaders considered FEL, but did not advance with it. Such a fascinating race where too little investment can have you beaten by a competitor who invests more, but investing & not getting a practical product quickly can also be fatal. Impossible to know who or what technology will win which makes watching your clear & exciting presentation that must have taken a long time to research & make so fascinating. Thank you for sharing!

author: @kris.yochev
text:
I feel the supposed IBM slide that's been going around is really at the core of it: "A computer cannot be held accountable and thus should not make a [management] decision". My thoughts on this are that at the end of the day, I'm putting this in front of customers, I want them to have the best experience possible. I remember seeing a quote somewhere "If 1 in every 100 products is defective, most business people would say it's a 1% failure rate, but from the point of view of a customer who bought a defective unit, it's a 100% failure rate". Basically, I think we as programmers have a duty of care that the code we ship works as best as we could possibly make it and we should've taken every reasonable measure to make sure bugs are caught and dealt with. That's why QA is there as well, that's why we write tests, that's why we review the code.

The reputation of the company/product is something that's hard to build but very easy to ruin.

I'm not against vibe coding/agentic engineering for one-offs, prototypes, and even internal tooling. Basically, if it doesn't end up shipped to a customer, it's absolutely fine. But for customer-facing code, the duty of care is there in my opinion.

author: @nopenope-j5s
text:
We have been talking about this a lot at our company recently. If you held human engineers to the same utter lack of standards that business desires from AI (just copy paste, make sure it passes, ship), I doubt there would be a huge gulf in speed.

Same for if you hold AI to the same standards as a human engineer. Write tests, worth the code, verify that it works, know the library you're using, etc, well... As you've said, it defeats the purpose in some ways.

I've had to send back tons of prs the past few months because people submit the first thing that falls out of Claude. I refuse to let it duplicate code. I refuse to let it just endlessly append until a function grows to be 5k lines.

My coworker did say just yesterday, "by the time I've fixed all these prs, I could have just coded it myself."

Indeed.

author: @scorch855
text:
I find the "vibe coding" end of the spectrum is really good for throwaway tools, quick prototypes, proof of concepts, testing assumptions, etc. 

But when it comes to building something which is intended to be used more than once (using AI tooling or otherwise) I'm a strong believer that you will get a much better result if you deliberately move slow and fix things.

The software engineering practices we have collectively developed over decades evolved in response to the problems encountered while working in that domain. Those problems don't just magical go away because we invented some clever new tools.

You wouldn't start driving 10x the speed limit without a seatbelt just because your new car has an autopilot feature. So by all means, test the limits of these new capabilities on a closed circuit with appropriate guardrails. But if you start operating your serious projects like that, don't be surprised when there are accidents.
video_title: Do Google engineers actually vibe code?

author: @patricknelson
text:
10:01 I’m at eBay (>10yrs) and I’ve always been and still very much am an advocate for this particular method, at least for _anything_ going into production. It is very much the norm on my team and the projects I manage. I think vibe coding still very much has its place, particularly for prototyping or just fun project stuff.

Basically: I don’t really care quite how you made the code per se, I just want to make sure that we thoroughly understand what it’s doing, make sure that it makes sense and so on. AI is still very much a factor throughout the process, but there’s ownership and, importantly, there’s understanding of what we’re shipping.

author: @PhilippensTube
text:
Maybe you could do a video on BamBuddy, an open souce (Bambu Lab) printer management and monitoring tool. Just to try it out (not out of conviction), I put my H2C in LAN/DEV mode and started using it. It works very well. You can monitor your prints, and even do that remotely. The user interface is a website, so you can use a VPN to your network to connect to it remotely, or other methods. There's also a sister project called SpoolBuddy, a spool management tool. It features a RFID tag reader and a spool database. There's also the scales (it's a hardware project, so you'll have to source some hardware and build it) to weigh the spools and thus keep track of what you have in store.
Tools like this helps people to use their Bambu Lab printer free from the cloud. I just wanted to try it; I don't have any problems with the Bambu cloud.
video_title: Bambu Lab A2L Review: Great Printer, Bad Timing

author: @briangerez8671
text:
I have three Bambu A1 printers, and there are always errors that are impossible to anticipate. Prints just freeze, ruining all the filament; there are moments when the extruder goes 'crazy' and starts slamming into the end of the X-axis mid-print; and bad bed levelings that are impossible to correct due to the lack of babysteps. On all three of my printers, the PEI sheets are scratched because the nozzle doesn't stop in time, scratching them. Or sometimes, it starts the printing process, and before moving to the center of the bed, it begins to drop below bed level, constantly slamming into the corner in an infinite loop that can only be fixed by turning the printer off.
It's worth noting that I use both official start G-codes and modified ones, and the exact same thing always happens regardless of the different commands or configurations, even with the machines properly calibrated. These are simply random errors that occur without warning.
video_title: Bambu Lab A2L Review: Great Printer, Bad Timing

text: I’ve had out of body experiences when I was 17. On the day of my senior prom, I was in a car accident and broke my back. I was in the hospital for over two months. I was put in a full body cast for six months. When I got out of hospital I was experiencing a lot of discomfort. To cope, I would envision myself floating up to the clouds and jumping from cloud to cloud. At night, I would wake up and find myself outside floating about 10 feet above the trees that lined the streets. I positioned myself above the center of the street. Below me were the streetlights and lights in the homes. I would travel above the streets and neighborhoods of my village. I did not have any bodily discomfort while doing this.

author: @waldemarcrosh9613
text:
Как сотрудник Ростелекома, расскажу вам сразу несколько вещей:
1. У автора видео с вероятностью 99% не Ростелеком, так как стоит сторонняя PON розетка, такую в нашей компании смогут установить лишь 1% выездных специалистов. Остальные обычно не знают, что так можно.
2. Сам провод от розетки до роутера в чёрной оплётке, он уличный, подороже чем серый и более редкий, ну прям маловероятно, что такой кинули.
3. Большая часть наших специалистов настолько ленивая, что соединили бы обычным штатным паткордом, там всё в одном месте, зачем мотать 15 метров провода, обжимать, бухтовать.
4. Провайдер любой отвечает до pon розетки, всё, что дальше это ваша зона ответственности. 
5. В основном в многоквартирных домах скорость на портах коммутатора уже ограничена программно 100 мегабит, так что если живёте в многоквартирном доме, 4 провода это норма, в подъезде вам всё равно подключат только 4, а у себя хоть все 8 обожмите, будет 4.

author: @Oni-games
text: Могут и не в коннекторе дело. У меня случай был. Уехал на 3 месяца, отключил интернет, стоял тариф 500мб/с, сказал я уезжаю. Вернулся, подключил заново, заплатил ту сумму. Подключили, пользовался неделю, потом чет скорость пропала. Замерил, выдает 100мб/с. Думаю че за дела, обратился в службу поддержки описал проблему. Игнор. Пока по адресу провайдера не съездил, не шевелились. Приехала бригада на след день. Проверили провод, коробку, комп. А потом выяснилось, абонента нового подключали, места в серверной не было на высокую скорость. Они рандомный провод переставили на 100мб/с а туда нового. Пошел ругаться, написал заявление, вернули неустойку. Короче отказался от провайдера…

author: @GoWatchArthurChristmas
text: One thing people tend to miss is that, most big brands didn't start with logos as simplistic as they are now. When brands start, standing out is a bigger priority, and when they are huge enough, recognition is a bigger priority. Which tends to mean brand logos shift in the Simplicity-Story scale over time. When we just look at the logos of the biggest brands, they seem too simple, because they have reached the level of "bigness" to warrant that level of "simpleness". A mom-and-pop restaurant round the corner is going to want a more "eyecatchy" logo than three spokes in a circle, that would largely go unnoticed, but it works for a major car brand with huge headquarters in one of the most famous cities in the world because what more "eyecatching" do they need? But to get to that spot of having a logo that looks so simple that everyone claims they could have done it too, they had to work through more detailed logos in their past too

author: @olbluetundra881
text: I'll stick with the ecoworthy for now. I have version 1, 2, and now 3. A total of 10. 4 in my portable workshop and 6 in my house for power outages. Probably like a lot of people i started with a ecoworthy 3500 kit. On both my home and trailer. 8 195 solar panels, a 3500 aio, and 1 100ah rack battery. I installed 1 on my portable work trailer with an ecoflow 3k generator that the inverter starts as needed. With 4 100ah rack batteries. I also used the extra 8 195 that I can plug into my trailer when I get home. Been running 2 years now with no issues. My home I bought the same system then upgraded it with 5 more ecoworthy rack batteries and the aptos panels you reviewed. A total of 12 415w panels. 3 sets of 3 to stay under the 150v max rating on the aoi. This system has performed flawlessly for just over 3 years now. I use as both backup and I run it like a generator 1 week a month on critical loads. I built my own automatic transfer switch to use for critical loads in my home, and my shop. I would really like for you to test a system like this. Show people the capabilities and limitations of a purchased system like this.

author: @hermannmaier
text: I like the idea of layered models: > L1 core: logic, math, physics, verified knowledge >> L2: mixture of experts >>> L3: customized models on top: company, organization, regional, national laws and rules, culturally diverse, trainable, subscribe/unsubscribable >>>> L4: personal modifications, preferences, routines, living on personal device, secure. Lower layers verify top layers and keep everything aligned. In theory you could then go the reverse route and collect anonymized or distilled top layers which are trained and modified at the company and user level and send them all back down to central servers, which crosscheck and train the next L1, L2 versions. Filtering up and filtering down.

author: @TechX1320
text: Fun fact, 2012 to 2014 North American Hyundai veloster's radios run on a very similar version of Windows NT. I haven't looked up the hardware in a very long time, but I believe it was some form of power PC. It really makes me wonder if you could run the radio software on a GameCube. That would be hilarious. The firmware is very difficult to find, but the ISO does exist, the only problem is you have to extract the ISO to an SD card, and then you put the SD card in the radio or a USB drive into the radio that's no bigger than 4 GB, with a folder named update and it installs everything

General PSA - If you are looking to connect to a TV and game for extended periods of time (road warriors living out of hotels, I'm talking to you.)

Pack a quart sized zip lock freezer bag, walk down to the hotel ice machine, fill it up with ice, then fill the bag up about a quarter of the way with water. Zip it tight and lay it on your phone (IP67 or higher phones only just in case of leaks.) Now you have a phone that will play at full speed a long time without temp throttling.

author: @danielsanichiban
text: Putting all of the logical and ethical issues of this aside, as a person who grew up in the 80s before there was any widespread surveillance or monitoring systems outside of CCTV in a Bank, the baseline feeling of existing, going anywhere, doing anything, exploring a creek or a forrest, riding a bike along a dirt track to nowhere, being unknown and alone, is completely different, there's a state of mind that I just can't reach anymore.   So many people may never know what it is to just be a living being sitting on a rock somewhere in the middle of this vast universe, and nothing more.  These days I feel more like a cow on a farm, with a yellow tag on my ear that reads B375, and that feeling is kind of inescapable.

author: @TerryParris-o3i
text: There is a problem here.  Nvidia NX1 is now called the Spark chip.  It is Arm and Cuda based.  Tesla as well is Arm based without Cuda or with Cuda has yet to be determined.  Both are paying for Arm technology annually.  Yes there are two different places these chips will be used.  However, BOTH Nvidia as well as Tesla have been providing code to their chips to the Linux kernel as well as the open source community.  Apple on the other hand is a CLOSED source ecosystem  They don't want any other operating system on their hardware.  Though the open source community is reverse engineering Apple hardware compatibility into Linux, Apple's software is based on BSD with a Mach kernel.  As BOTH Nvidia and Tesla commit to the Linux (open source) ecosystem (kernel), they will have more access to applications that even Apple doesn't have an answer for.  Microsoft with it's Windows on Arm has got the problem mentioned as still having a lot of X86 based applications.  Linux here will be the winner as most of the applications that are open sourced can be compiled for Arm.  So where does that leave any of the players?

author: @Kevins-Philippine-Retirement
text: I love in the rural Philippines (no fibre optic Internet) and am currently using Starlink. I teach English online using video conferencing. My speeds are usually 200+DL and about 20UL. In really bad weather it may go as slow as 60DL /10UL. Before Starlink, I used cellular internet with a MIMO directional antenna on a 20' mast. The towers for all 3 providers here are only 1km away line of sight, but I'm sure they all have the power turned down, as there is just 0 to 3 bars on the phone. With my setup I was getting about 30 to 60DL/3 to 10UL. It was marginally satisfactory for video conferencing.  Starlink costs about $67USD/mo, and one of the cellular providers (Smart) cost just $21USD/mo. The biggest problem using the cellular option was quite often the cellular service would just die for an unpredictable time, and during power outages, within a few minutes the service was gone (no power backup). So, sadly Starlink is my only choice. As long as I can keep fuel in my generator I'm good to go. It doesn't rely on the ground infrastructure.
video_title: The Alternative to Starlink You Never Knew Existed

author: @GainingDespair
text:
I've only ever owned one Razer product and it was a wireless gaming mouse. I replaced it because I got tired of their approach to software updates: you either let their telemetry run 24/7 or you start a countdown timer until their software refuses to function without an internet connection. I would be playing a game and my mouse macros/button remapping would stop working mid-session. It happened fairly frequently, normally I just had to close the app and relaunch, but it would also break due to a pending update not being installed. 

So between the software breaking randomly and locking you out if you didn't install Synapse updates immediately, it would also block you after 2 weeks of no internet connection. I had it blocked at the firewall to cut off its connection, which only broke it further. I'm done with their software, not worth the frustration. I don't want a 50% chance my mouse macros won't work just because I hadn't turned my PC on in a week and didn't prioritize their software update above everything else.

author: @chrisball3778
text: I knew the twist because I visited a fort in Malta where they have one of Armstrong's inventions- the 100-ton gun. It's the biggest muzzle-loading cannon ever created and it's one of the most insanely steampunk things I've ever seen. It could fire a shell that weighed as much as a small car 6 kilometres. It was loaded with hydraulics and it's trajectories were calculated with slide rules. In the 1870's Armstrong offered it to the Royal Navy, but they turned it down. So he sold some to the Italian Navy. Then he went back to the British and pointed out that the Italians had a bigger gun than them now. So they had to buy some as well. There are two of them left- the one in Malta that I saw, and another one in Gibraltar.

author: @Handcannon77
text:
I dont really quite understand why they would release the 10xxx and 11xxx series chips back to back..? I mean, in all honesty the 9950x3d didnt come out all that long ago and is a beast of a chip. Im sure the performance gain will be fairly decent..but idk, I think a lot of people who are just looking at gaming with it and didnt buy this generation, will either look at the 10 and 11 series as too expensive for performance that they kind of dont really need, when they can opt for the cheaper, but still incredible 9800x3d or 9950x3d and get all the power theyll need for it.

 Now if the performance gains are sizeable enough, and the price isnt stupid high(maybe slightly higher than what the 9000 series released at) I could see even people who DID buy this generation be willing to just sell their 9000 series and upgrade, but if were talking $800+ for the CPU itself, it will be a niche card for the people who can afford it or the people who really need that level of performance. Imo the 10 and 11 x3d series chips might start reaching a point of CPU performance that is simply overkill for most gamers, and only see decent performance jumps compared to the 9000 series in the MOST CPU bound games out there(which isnt that many). Im also just skeptical of how releasing  the 10 and 11 series like that wont eat into sales of one or the other...I mean theres a reason why it usually isnt done.

author: @shantanusaha9746
text:
My school district, the NYC Public Schools, has 1400 schools, but we did not set up a system-wide MDM until COVID massively expanded the number of devices in student hands. So now, five years on from the year of COVID remote learning, the bill is coming due. I manage the tech in my school, and so this spring we're recalling all student devices so we can get a handle on the devices we actually have vs. the devices that our system says that are in students' possession. The devices remaining in the wind will get the Lost Mode treatment -- they will have banners announcing that they need to be returned to us (either to my school in particular or to NYCPS in general) for the next year or so, until they get put on the permanently lost pile and get removed from MDM.

Until recently, every time I send devices for recycling (there's a city-wide process for this), it didn't matter because the devices were not under MDM. The new process is that we have to provide the recycler with a list of serial numbers or asset tags so that they can remove the devices from MDM. I plan to follow up by putting that list through a bulk removal process myself a month or so after the recycler picks them up.

author: @Piipperi800
text:
To correct the video a little, the issue isn't exactly the MDM, it's Apple Business Manager or Apple School Manager (depending on the org).
ABM / ASM is what registers the device with an MDM (such as JAMF or Microsoft Intune), and you have to release (aka remove) the device from ABM / ASM in order to properly remove it from the organization systems like MDM. If you simply remove the device only from the MDM, it's really easy for an organization to accidentally re-add the device to an MDM that was already removed from there. This is what sometimes results in devices suddenly becoming MDM locked despite not being like that earlier. Releasing products from ABM / ASM is really easy as well, I don't think there's much of an issue with the usability of it. However, very often at large corporations, the 1st level help desk people do not have access to ABM / ASM, so there's that too.

Also, there are cases of stolen devices with MDM locks on them, in which case it does and is supposed to act like iCloud lock would (to my understanding). These are of course more uncommon than companies just forgetting to release the device from ABM / ASM.

There unfortunately probably isn't much that can be done, other than hoping you can get the company to properly release the device. It's really difficult for Apple to also do anything, I'd assume companies wouldn't want Apple just automatically releasing devices from their ABM / ASM enviroments. Althrough, I have heard of some cases where maybe Apple can remove it if you have the original purchase receipt (but as an end user, there's no way you're getting that lmao)
(MDM locks have literally also been an issue even with officially Apple Refurbished items. I remember some YouTuber (maybe it was Quinn?) getting a laptop from the refurb store, and it was locked to an MDM)

author: @billgallop
text:
I’ve worked in the industry for a long time and this is simple to deal with, in exactly the same way that the guys that buy your old iPhone etc. ensure that Activation Lock is removed. 

The companies buying old stock from corporations give them two prices. A locked (scrap) price and an unlocked price. You don’t pay for the device until you have checked it over, assessed the condition and if it is MDM locked or not. If it’s locked, just like if the screen is smashed, they only get scrap value. 

It is not down to Apple to remove MDM lock. MDM lock works through a chain of trust and Apple will not remove it without proof that it no longer belongs to that organisation, and that proof is the organisation releasing it in Apple Business. Something that takes only a few seconds.

Simple Chocolate Chip Cookies 🍪

Ingredients

1/2 cup (115g) butter (soft)

1/2 cup sugar

1/2 cup brown sugar

1 egg

1 teaspoon vanilla essence

1 and 1/2 cups flour

1/2 teaspoon baking soda

1/4 teaspoon salt

1 cup chocolate chips


Steps

1. Preheat oven to 180°C.


2. Mix butter, sugar, and brown sugar well.


3. Add egg and vanilla, then mix again.


4. In another bowl, mix flour, baking soda, and salt.


5. Add dry ingredients to the wet mixture.


6. Stir in chocolate chips.


7. Make small balls of dough and place on a baking tray.


8. Bake for 10–12 minutes until golden brown.


9. Let cool and enjoy!

video_title: What Is LG Thinking? LG C6 OLED Review

author: @improvedgarage
text: I may be in the minority here, but I'm at the point where I don't even want a smart TV anymore. I just want a TV with a couple HDMI inputs that has a bright, colorful screen that's accurate. I have an LG, a Sony, and a Samsung. All of them have UIs that get crappier over time. We live in a world where computers have a 3- to 5-year life expectancy, and building them into our TVs that historically have had much longer expectancies than that seems short-sighted.  What's worse is the way these manufacturers make it so that you have to go through their internal menu system and their smart apps to even get to the HDMI input.  Build me a TV that is bright and accurate, and let me bring my own source. I'll swap out my Apple TV every three years as needed.

author: @donotneed2250
text:
For me it's keep Do Not Disturb on and set to only allow contacts to ring through.  If something manages to get through I block it.  I let unrecognized numbers go to voicemail and if they don't leave a message I block it.  If they leave a message and I don't like it I'll block them.

If I'm in the mood when the phone rings and the house is quiet I'll answer and won't say anything.  Or I just may answer in Korean.  Either way it confuses them and they hang up and don't call back.

author: @aynaudhebert
text:
You've overlooked an important factor.  Most of my spam calls report inactive numbers, which if subsequently called, trigger a telephone recording the the number is not in service. 
This is accomplished by the caller by calling through a computer, using Voice Over Internet Protocol (VOIP).  VOIP software allows caller to enter whatever number is desired to appear on recipient's phone.
As for political / polling calls prior to elections, your voter registration info is public record. Do you need a phone number to be eligible to vote? Of course not. When you registered, did you provide your phone number? Probably. Consider changing your registration to have no phone number

author: @SCUFPE
text:
Hola gente! Hemos leído sus comentarios y queremos explicarles un poco del procedimiento que realizamos. 
Se lo comentamos al cliente y el nos autorizo a hacer los cambios. 
Al abrirlo, realizamos una limpieza interna a los compenentes, allí tuvimos que retirar la “boca”, y adentro estaba la estructura del mando (La cosa negra que ven) la cual estaba dividida, como se ve en 00:25, está estructura es fundamental ya que separa los gatillos físicos y cables en el mando para que no hagan falso contacto en la placa.

Además, encontramos insectos en el botón, por lo que era necesario abrirlo.

Lamentablemente, la pieza estaba reseca y se quebró al retirarla. Le 
pegamos la pieza original y también imprimimos dos opciones nuevas para que pueda elegir (una fina y otra reforzada). En el video se muestra la más fina.

Estamos para darles la mejor solución 🫡

author: @alpcns
text: I worked on these devices for quite some time a few years back. It's a ORR (Oxygen Reduction Reaction) device - the alloy block is most likely a Mg alloy, possibly with Zinc. The membrane is mounted such that oxygen can permeate the membrane but the electrolyte (water and salt) cannot. They're still producing a whole line of these products for Australian company at the time, assembly in China. This is likely a Chinese clone of it. There have been novel designs of the electrolyte container such that lamps/lights/radios/USB devices et cetera can be used in any position without leakage. The challenge at the time was in the electronics to boost the ORR reaction voltage and prevent inevitable passivation of the Mg alloy, which would kill the reaction prematurely. Another challenge was containing the Mg "sludge": a result of the ORR reaction with the Mg. Salt "seepage" along wiring into the device itself was another issue that has been solved at the time.

author: @zh84
text: In re determining the metal: fill a small cup to the brim until it is overflowing, then drop in a lump of metal and measure the volume of water that overflows. Then weigh the lump to get its density. This is famously how Archimedes determined whether a crown was pure gold or an alloy. I don't know if you could get enough accuracy, but according to Google the density of zinc is 7.1g per cubic centimetre, whereas aluminium is 2.7g per cubic centimetre - less than half as much. Indeed, with that level of difference you might be to measure the lump of metal, calculate its rough volume, then weigh it.

author: @SeanBZA
text: I got, many years ago, a few of the emergency light units from life rafts, which were recertified every 5 years, before being packed back into the SA220 Super frelon helicopters. these little light units were part of the kit, and, as they could not test them, they simply discorded them completely, and put new ones in. Inside a green HDPE case were the 2 electrodes, one being a stainless steel one, and the other being a mgnesium alloy of some sort, with crimped electrodes that were sealed inside the HDPE wall during assembly. 2m of basically thin alarm wire, 0.5mm 7 strand PVC copper wire, which led to the actual lamp itself, a single 1 cell torch lamp in a sealed acrylic housing, with a hook to hang it on the top of the self inflating life raft. Instructions were, in case of ditchin, were to inflate the raft by using the lanyard, and at night dip the green plastic in a pouch, after filling it with a little sea water, and it would be guaranteed to operate at least 20 night, though you pulled it out during the day. Tried it, and it was certainly working, but a firefly does provide more light, though at night even this small light was usable, and the more important part of the kit this was in was the pack of 6 smoke flares, the 6 red flares, and the 6 packs of brilliant green yellow dye, that could be used to make a tiny red orange raft visible in a very large ocean. Plus a mirror, a whistle, and 6 bars of what could be called edible, along with a single liter of water in a very thick bag, all packed into a plastic inner box about the size of a 2l ice cream pack. Water tasted like plastic, and those bars not even the rats would eat them, and the guard dogs sniffed, then growled at them. But those flares were fun NYE though, even if they were overshadowed by the big ship ones being lit off from the harbour, and the one area resembling an active war zone, from all the flashes and blasts.

author: @Lardzor
text: This reminds me of how they powered the electronic proximity fuses in military shells. They needed a way to prevent the batteries from self-discharging over time as the shells were kept in long-term storage. Their solution was to separate the electrolyte from the battery in an ampule. When the shell was fired, the ampule would break and flood the battery providing the power necessary for the electronics.

text: UK  phones are also different to US phones, we don’t have the antenna, because mmWave is not widely deployed, so we don’t need a mmWave antenna. we rely on Sub 6-GHz 5G. We also have a sim card slot, assumingly because most people use physical sims, and most providers don’t allow you to use on device conversion, some providers still don’t support eSIM at all, and it would be slow. Also our shutter sound plays when the phone isn’t on silent, doesn’t play when it’s on silent.

text:
The reason the battery is better is because it does not support millimeter wave 5G. Meaning that the internet at least here in the US if you imported one would be very slow. 

Also answers the battery question, the downside of the millimeter wave 5G antenna is that it does deplete battery quicker. The US model has a bigger battery but it drains quicker because of that stronger antenna
